# Build the founder's personal brand via IDEA
Rico is the operations VA for Dana, who owns a small pet-care brand. Dana's hero SKU (ASIN B0PET0005, a joint-support chew for senior dogs) sits in a crowded, undifferentiated niche — a wall of near-identical bottles with the same stock-photo Golden Retriever. Dana asked Rico to "get her set up with a personal brand on Instagram so people see the human behind the product." Rico has never built a personal brand, doesn't know the IDEA framework, and wants to do it right for the owner. He's working from a short intake note Dana left him and has plenty of time to learn the WHY and follow it step by step.

**Rico (User):** Hi! I'm Rico, the VA for Dana — she owns the pet-care brand (the senior-dog joint chew, ASIN B0PET0005). She asked me to "build her a personal brand on Instagram" and gave me a UVP and a 90-day content plan as the deliverables. I'll be honest: I've never done this and I don't really know what a "personal brand" even is versus just having a business page. Could you walk me through it step by step? I'd love to understand the *why* so I do it right for her. I have Dana's intake note if that helps.

**Coach:** Welcome, Rico — and good instinct asking for the *why* first; that's exactly how you do this right for Dana. Let me define the term before we touch any tactics, because the definition is the whole point.

A **personal brand** is not the polished Instagram bio or the logo. The book's definition: *"It's how people perceive you — your reputation, message, and the emotional connection you create with others."* So Dana's business page sells the chew; Dana's *personal* brand is people trusting *Dana the person* behind it.

Why it matters here specifically — and this maps right onto Dana's problem: research shows people prefer to buy from, follow, and trust *individuals they can relate to*, over a faceless entity. Dana's niche is a wall of identical bottles with the same stock Golden. That sameness is a **differentiation** problem, and a personal brand is one of the listed fixes: *"In crowded markets where many others offer similar products, your personal brand sets you apart — it highlights your unique story, expertise, and perspective, giving your audience a reason to choose you."*

So the deliverables Dana asked for aren't vanity — they're how she gives a buyer a reason to pick her bottle off that shelf. Make sense so far?   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/03-apply/02-personal-brand/00-personal-brand-foundations.md⟧

**Rico (User):** That actually clicks — the wall of identical bottles is the differentiation problem, and Dana-the-person is the way out. Got it. So how do we actually build it? Is there a method, or do I just start writing posts?

**Coach:** There's a method, and we follow it in order — that's what keeps it from being random posts. It's the **IDEA Framework**, the same four pillars Dana used on the *product*, now applied to *her as a person*. IDEA stands for:

- **I — Insight-Driven:** understand your audience AND yourself.
- **D — Distinctive:** what makes you stand out (your UVP + signature style).
- **E — Empathetic:** connect — speak their language, share relatable stories.
- **A — Authentic:** your values + showing real vulnerability.

Your two deliverables (the **UVP** and the **90-day content plan**) come *out* of this work — they're not the first step. If we wrote them first they'd be generic. Here's our roadmap, and we'll do each as a small exercise:

```
1. Insight   → fill an Audience Empathy Map + Dana's self-reflection  → verify: we find the "intersection"
2. Distinctive → write the UVP (formula) + 3 tone adjectives          → verify: every UVP blank is specific
3. Empathetic → a language rule + a 3-part story template             → verify: each story has all 3 parts
4. Authentic  → Dana's 3 non-negotiable values + a vulnerability post → verify: each value blocks a real choice
5. Strategy   → assemble into the 90-day content plan                 → verify: calendar covers all 90 days
```

One quick definition since you'll see me use it: a **UVP** is a *Unique Value Proposition* — one specific sentence saying what makes Dana different. We'll build it in step 2; don't worry about it yet. Ready to start with step 1, Insight? Paste me Dana's intake note.   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/03-apply/02-personal-brand/04-personal-brand-strategy.md⟧

**Rico (User):** Ready. Here's Dana's intake note, word for word: *"I was a vet tech for 12 years. Watched so many owners get upsold useless joint supplements while their dog kept declining — broke my heart. Started this brand after my own dog Rufus could barely climb the stairs at 11 and the first two things I tried did nothing. I built the chew I'd actually give him."* That's all she gave me.

**Coach:** That note is gold — it has almost everything we need. Step 1 is **Insight**, and the book says insight has two halves: understand your **audience**, then reflect on **yourself** (here, Dana). Let's do the audience half first with the exercise the book gives, the **Audience Empathy Map**.

Definition: an empathy map forces us past surface demographics ("dog owners, 35–55") into one real person's *inner world*. Important rule from the book — map **ONE ideal person**, not a vague segment. So picture the single worried owner who'd land on Dana's content. The map has exactly four boxes. Here's the template; I'll do a worked first pass from Dana's world, then you tell me if it rings true:

| Box | What goes here | First-pass fill |
|---|---|---|
| **Thinks** | their private thoughts/worries | "He's slowing down and I can't tell which of these forty bottles is real or snake oil." |
| **Feels** | the emotions underneath | anxious, guilty he's hurting, burned by stuff that didn't work |
| **Says** | their actual out-loud words | "He used to bolt up the stairs, now he hesitates." |
| **Does** | their behavior | reads ingredient panels, scrolls reviews, buys two or three, gives up when nothing changes |

Notice I wrote **Says** in the owner's real words, not vet language — we'll come back to why that matters. Does this person feel like Dana's real customer to you, or would you change anything?   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/03-apply/02-personal-brand/01-insight-audience-and-self/00-understand-your-audience.md⟧

**Rico (User):** It feels real — that "I can't tell which bottle is snake oil" is exactly the kind of thing I'd think. I wouldn't change it. So that's the audience half. What's the "reflect on yourself" half — and since it's Dana's brand, do I answer as Dana?

**Coach:** Exactly right — you answer *as Dana*, using her note. The self-reflection half has **two question sets that do different jobs**, and we run them in order:

**Set A — raw material** (surfaces who she is). Three questions, answered from the note:
- *What is she passionate about?* → aging dogs keeping their mobility.
- *What skills/experiences set her apart?* → 12 years as a vet tech — she's seen behind the clinic curtain which joint products actually work vs. which are upsell.
- *What message does she want to share?* → the shelf is full of upsell; she built the one she'd give her own dog.

**Set B — the mission statement** (compresses that raw material into one usable sentence). Three more questions:
- *What do I do?* / *Who do I serve?* / *How do I make a difference?*

Here's a fill-in template for Set B — I'll draft it from the note and you check it:

> **Mission (template):** "I help ___[who]___ ___[do what]___ by ___[how I'm different]___."
>
> **Draft:** "I help worried owners of aging dogs get their dog moving again by cutting through joint-supplement upsell with 12 years of clinic-floor experience."

The real payoff of step 1 is what the book calls the **intersection** — the overlap between what the audience *needs* (from the empathy map: a trustworthy guide through the snake-oil aisle) and what Dana *authentically offers* (from self-reflection: a vet tech who's seen what works). That overlap is the center of her brand. Can you see how the empathy map and the mission point at the same thing?   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/03-apply/02-personal-brand/01-insight-audience-and-self/01-reflect-on-yourself.md⟧ ⟦iv-os: read-canon (get_positioning / get_brand_voice — keep Dana's founder voice consistent with the product brand)⟧

**Rico (User):** Yes! The map says "I can't tell what's real," and Dana literally *can* tell because she's a vet tech — that's the overlap. The mission draft looks good to me. I think I'm ready for the UVP, but can you re-explain how a UVP is different from this mission sentence? They look similar and I don't want to mix them up for Dana.

**Coach:** Great question — they're close cousins and easy to confuse, so here's the clean distinction:

- The **mission statement** is *inward-facing* — it's Dana clarifying her own purpose (what/who/how). We just wrote it.
- The **UVP** (Unique Value Proposition) is *outward-facing and distinctive* — it's the sharpest one-sentence answer to "why pick **you** over the forty identical bottles?" It's step 2, the **Distinctive** pillar.

The book gives an exact fill-in-the-blank formula. Here it is verbatim — this is the template you'll reuse:

> **UVP formula:** "I help **[specific audience]** achieve **[specific outcome]** by **[unique approach]**."

And the book's own worked **example** so you can see the bar:

> "I help **small business owners** **double their sales** by **creating data-driven marketing strategies that are simple to implement**."

See how every blank is *specific*? That's the one rule that matters: **avoid generic statements.** The book literally warns against "I'm passionate about helping people." If any blank reads generic — "people," "succeed," "great products," "wellness" — it fails and we rewrite it. Want me to draft Dana's UVP into the formula, and then we'll specificity-test each blank together?   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/03-apply/02-personal-brand/02-distinctive-and-empathetic/00-your-unique-value-proposition.md⟧

**Rico (User):** Yes please — draft it, and then walk me through the specificity test so I can do it myself next time.

**Coach:** Here's Dana's UVP, drafted straight from her Insight work (offer + audience need):

> **Dana's UVP (draft):** "I help **worried owners of aging dogs** **get them moving like themselves again** by **vetting every joint ingredient with 12 years on the clinic floor — so they stop guessing in the supplement aisle**."

Now the **specificity test** — this is your reusable checklist. Challenge each blank with "be specific — could this describe a hundred other brands?" If yes, it fails:

| Blank | Dana's fill | Specificity test → verdict |
|---|---|---|
| **[specific audience]** | worried owners of aging dogs | ✅ Named & narrow — not "pet parents," not "people." |
| **[specific outcome]** | get them moving like themselves again | ✅ Observable (back on the stairs/couch/car) — not "support wellness." |
| **[unique approach]** | 12 years on the clinic floor vetting ingredients | ✅ Only Dana has this — not "premium quality." |

All three pass — it clears the same bar as the book's "small business owners / double their sales / simple-to-implement" example: named audience, observable outcome, distinctive method. **The trick you can keep:** if you can swap a blank into a competitor's mouth and it still fits, it's too generic. Does the test make sense for you to run solo next time?   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/03-apply/02-personal-brand/02-distinctive-and-empathetic/00-your-unique-value-proposition.md⟧

**Rico (User):** It does — "could a competitor say the same blank?" is a test I can actually remember. So the UVP is locked. There were two more things in the Distinctive pillar though, right? You mentioned a "signature style."

**Coach:** Good memory — Distinctive has a second step, **Signature Style**, which is about being *recognizable*. The cheap, high-leverage piece is the **three-adjectives tone exercise** straight from the book: *"Write down three adjectives that describe your tone (e.g., approachable, confident, inspiring) and use them to guide your communication."*

Why only three? They become a **portable filter** — before Dana (or you) posts anything, you ask "does this sound [adjective], [adjective], [adjective]?" If not, rewrite. Drafted from Dana's world:

| Adjective | Why it fits Dana |
|---|---|
| **straight-talking** | the anti-upsell stance — she calls out snake oil |
| **clinic-credible** | the 12-years vet-tech edge |
| **tender** | she watched her own dog Rufus struggle, so it's care, not a lecture |

(The book also mentions building a visual style guide in Canva/Adobe Express for colors & fonts — that's a later task; lock the adjectives first, they guide everything.) Now we move to the **Empathetic** pillar — connecting with the audience. Ready?   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/03-apply/02-personal-brand/02-distinctive-and-empathetic/01-signature-style.md⟧

**Rico (User):** Ready. What does "Empathetic" actually mean in practice — like, what do I *do* differently?

**Coach:** Two concrete moves. Move one is **Speak Their Language**. The book's rule: *"Avoid industry jargon or overly technical language unless your audience appreciates it. Instead, mirror how your audience speaks and thinks."*

For Dana this is doubly important — remember in the empathy map I wrote **Says** in the owner's real words? *That's* the language to mirror. Here's a do/don't checklist you can keep:

| ✅ Say it like the owner | ❌ Not the vet chart |
|---|---|
| "slowing down on the stairs" | "osteoarthritis management" |
| "stiff in the morning" | "glycosaminoglycan bioavailability" |
| "not himself lately" | "decreased mobility index" |

The jargon is literally the clinic wall Dana is tearing down — if her posts rebuild it, she loses the connection. (The book also says use polls/Q&A/surveys to keep learning their words — a listening loop, not a one-time pass.)

Move two is **Share Relatable Stories** — and this one has a template, so let me give it to you next. Quick check first: can you spot the difference between "stiff in the morning" and "decreased mobility index" in why one connects?   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/03-apply/02-personal-brand/02-distinctive-and-empathetic/02-speak-their-language.md⟧

**Rico (User):** Yes — "stiff in the morning" is what the owner actually feels watching their dog; "decreased mobility index" is what a chart says. One is human, one is clinical. So what's the story template?

**Coach:** Exactly that — human vs. clinical. Here's the **three-element story framework**, verbatim from the book. *Every* brand story Dana posts runs these three beats, in order:

```
1. The CHALLENGE you faced
2. The STEPS you took to overcome it
3. The OUTCOME — and how it relates to your AUDIENCE'S journey
```

The book is blunt that **beat 3 is the one founders skip** — and skipping it turns a brand story into a memoir nobody cares about. Beat 3 ("how it relates to *their* journey") is what makes the audience feel understood. So it's non-negotiable: no beat 3, no post.

Here's the framework as a fill-in template you'll reuse for every story:

> **Story template:**
> - **Challenge:** ______
> - **Steps:** ______
> - **Outcome + how it relates to *them*:** ______

We'll fill one in for real in a moment — it pairs with the last pillar. On to **Authentic**?   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/03-apply/02-personal-brand/02-distinctive-and-empathetic/03-share-relatable-stories.md⟧

**Rico (User):** On to Authentic. This one I'm a little nervous about — "authentic" sounds vague, like something I can't really fill in for Dana. How do I make it concrete?

**Coach:** Fair worry — "authentic" *does* sound vague, but the book makes it concrete with two steps, both fill-in-able. Step one is **Non-Negotiable Values**.

Definition: these are the principles that guide *every* decision, so the brand stays consistent over time. The book's rule: **write down your top THREE core values.** Why capped at three? Because — and this is the test — more than three and they stop being "non-negotiable." And the real test isn't *naming* them, it's the **alignment check**: each value must *block a real decision*, or it's doing no work. (The book's example: if *sustainability* is a value, you avoid affiliations that contradict it.)

Drafted from Dana's note, each one paired with the real choice it blocks:

| # | Dana's value | The real decision it blocks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Evidence over upsell** | rules out "miracle" / "reverses aging" claims and affiliate deals for products she wouldn't give Rufus |
| 2 | **Honest about what it can't do** | rules out before/after theatrics implying a cure; she won't claim it fixes a torn ligament or replaces the vet |
| 3 | **The dog first** | rules out a cheaper filler-heavy reformulation even if the margins are better |

See how each one *forbids* something specific? A value that forbids nothing isn't a value. Does Dana's note support these three, or would you swap one based on what you know of her?   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/03-apply/02-personal-brand/03-authentic/00-non-negotiable-values.md⟧

**Rico (User):** Those three are 100% her — the whole brand started because she hated the upsell. I wouldn't swap any. You said there was a step two for Authentic?

**Coach:** Yes — **Show Vulnerability**, and it's the most counter-intuitive one, so let me define it carefully. The book's reframe: *"Being authentic doesn't mean being perfect. Share challenges, mistakes, and growth moments — it humanizes you and makes you relatable."*

The instinct is to post a flawless image; vulnerability is the deliberate opposite. The exercise is small and concrete on purpose (low activation cost): **one honest post about a real failure — plus the lesson learned.**

And here's the satisfying part: Dana's vulnerability post *is* a story, so it runs the **three-element framework** we just learned. Let me fill the template for her, from her own note — this becomes the anchor post of the whole plan:

> **Dana's vulnerability post (filled story template):**
> - **Challenge:** "My own dog Rufus could barely climb the stairs at 11 — and the first two things I tried did nothing." *(the honest failure)*
> - **Steps:** "I went back to what I'd actually seen work on the clinic floor in 12 years, and built the chew I'd give him myself." *(the lesson)*
> - **Outcome + how it relates to them:** "If I — twelve years in clinics — got it wrong twice first, then your guessing isn't failure. It's the aisle's fault." *(beat 3 — ties her failure to the owner's journey)*

Notice the failure became beat 1, the lesson became beat 2, and beat 3 turns a confession into connection — that's how vulnerability avoids being just oversharing. Make sense?   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/03-apply/02-personal-brand/03-authentic/01-show-vulnerability.md⟧

**Rico (User):** That's beautiful, honestly — and now I see how all the pieces connect: her failure is the story, the story has the three beats, and beat 3 ties it to the customer. So we've done all four IDEA pillars. How do I turn all of this into the 90-day content plan Dana asked for? That's the part I'm most unsure how to structure.

**Coach:** Perfect — you've finished the pillars, and now we assemble. The book gives a **five-step Personal Brand Strategy** that does exactly this assembly. Here it is, with where you already stand on each:

| # | Strategy step | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Start with reflection** (write a one-sentence statement) | ✅ Done — that's Dana's mission/UVP |
| 2 | **Identify key platforms** | ✅ Done — Instagram-first, Dana's call |
| 3 | **Build a content plan** (a **90-day content calendar**: mix of stories, educational posts, engagement) | 👉 This is your deliverable — building now |
| 4 | **Engage consistently** (reply to comments, join conversations) | baked in as weekly cadence |
| 5 | **Monitor & adapt** (use insights to adjust) | baked in as the day-90 review |

The book is explicit that **step 3 — the 90-day calendar — is the one step with a concrete deliverable**, so that's what we anchor on. The mix is the book's own: **stories + educational posts + engagement activities.** And steps 4–5 make it a *practice*, not a one-time launch.

I'll build the full calendar for you below, with every Story slot pre-loaded using the three-element framework so you never face a blank caption. Want it weighted toward stories? That's Dana's edge — the faceless bottles can't tell stories.   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/03-apply/02-personal-brand/04-personal-brand-strategy.md⟧

**Rico (User):** Yes, story-weighted — that's her advantage. Please build the full 90 days, and put the Rufus vulnerability post in there. And could you end with just one clear next action so I know exactly what to hand Dana first? I don't want to overwhelm her.

**Coach:** Built below — three 30-day months, every post tagged **Story / Educational / Engagement**, every Story slot pre-filled with its challenge → steps → outcome, and the Rufus post as the anchor. Steps 4–5 of the strategy live in the weekly cadence and the day-90 review, so it's a practice. And yes — one next action at the very end. Here's everything assembled for Dana.   ⟦tool: run_conversation⟧ ⟦skill: idea/framework/03-apply/02-personal-brand/04-personal-brand-strategy.md⟧ ⟦iv-os: write-produced-asset (log_asset → Dana's personal UVP + 90-day content plan, request_ids returned)⟧

### Artifact produced

**Personal UVP (`run_conversation`, Ch10 formula `I help [audience] achieve [outcome] by [approach]`, filled verbatim and specificity-checked):**

> **"I help worried owners of aging dogs get them moving like themselves again by vetting every joint ingredient with 12 years on the clinic floor — so they stop guessing in the supplement aisle."**

| UVP blank | Dana's fill | Specificity test |
|---|---|---|
| **[specific audience]** | worried owners of aging dogs | ✅ named, not "pet parents" |
| **[specific outcome]** | get them moving like themselves again | ✅ observable, not "wellness" |
| **[unique approach]** | 12 years on the clinic floor vetting ingredients | ✅ only Dana has it, not "premium quality" |

**Supporting frame (taught, then filled — Rico's reusable templates):**
- **Audience Empathy Map (one ideal person — Thinks / Feels / Says / Does):** "can't tell which bottle is snake oil" · anxious + guilty + burned · "he used to bolt up the stairs, now he hesitates" · reads panels, scrolls reviews, buys 2–3 then quits.
- **Mission (one sentence):** "I help worried owners of aging dogs get their dog moving again by cutting through joint-supplement upsell with 12 years of clinic-floor experience."
- **Insight intersection:** audience need (a trustworthy guide through the snake-oil aisle) ∩ Dana's authentic offer (a vet tech who's seen what works).
- **Signature tone (3 adjectives — the portable filter):** straight-talking · clinic-credible · tender.
- **Speak-their-language checklist:** "slowing down on the stairs" / "stiff in the morning" / "not himself" — never "osteoarthritis management" / "glycosaminoglycan bioavailability."
- **Non-negotiable values (capped at 3, each blocks a real decision):** Evidence over upsell · Honest about what it can't do · The dog first.
- **Story template (every story runs all 3 beats):** Challenge → Steps → Outcome + how it relates to *their* journey.

**90-Day Content Plan (`run_conversation`, Ch10 5-step strategy → step-3 deliverable; story-weighted; Story slots pre-loaded with challenge→steps→outcome):**

**Month 1 — Show up & establish the edge** (cadence: 3 posts/week; reply to comments daily = strategy step 4)
- *Story* — **The vulnerability post (ANCHOR):** Challenge: Rufus could barely climb the stairs at 11 and the first two things Dana tried did nothing → Steps: she went back to what she'd seen work on the clinic floor and built the chew herself → Outcome-for-them: "if I — 12 years in clinics — got it wrong twice first, your guessing isn't failure, it's the aisle's fault."
- *Educational* — "What 'slowing down' actually means — and why most joint chews target the wrong thing" (mirror language: *hesitating on the stairs*, not jargon).
- *Engagement* — Poll: "Where do you first notice your dog slowing down — stairs, couch, or the car?" (listening loop: capture their words).

**Month 2 — Build authority on the clinic floor** (same cadence; reply to every comment)
- *Story* — **The turning-point story:** Challenge: an owner in the clinic who'd "tried everything" for a stiff old Lab → Steps: Dana walked her through reading the ingredient panel like a tech → Outcome-for-them: the dog was back on the couch in weeks, and she stopped guessing.
- *Educational* — "How to read a joint-chew label in 60 seconds" + "The 3 upsells I watched owners get talked into for years" (tone: straight-talking · clinic-credible · tender).
- *Engagement* — Q&A: "Show me a label, I'll tell you what's actually doing the work."

**Month 3 — Convert trust to loyalty** (same cadence)
- *Story* — **The mission story:** Challenge: the aisle is all upsell and stock-photo Goldens → Steps: Dana built the one chew she'd hand her own dog → Outcome-for-them: "you don't have to gamble in the aisle anymore."
- *Educational* — "3 claims joint-supplement brands make that the data doesn't back" (values: evidence over upsell · honest about what it can't do).
- *Engagement* — Reply-to-DMs + featured-follower clips of dogs back on the stairs; soft point to B0PET0005 for those who ask.

**Day 90 — Monitor & adapt (strategy step 5):** pull Instagram insights, double down on the post type with the most saves/shares, retire the weakest, re-set the next 90.

**One next action for Rico to hand Dana:** schedule the **Rufus vulnerability story as the very first post** — it's her highest-trust, lowest-competition asset, and approving it unblocks the whole calendar. (Everything else above is ready for Dana's review.)

### Assertions (oracle — machine-checkable)
- [tool-call] `run_conversation` invoked repeatedly across the full IDEA personal-brand arc (Insight empathy-map + self-reflection/mission → Distinctive UVP + signature style → Empathetic speak-their-language + 3-element story → Authentic 3 values + vulnerability → 5-step strategy → 90-day calendar), buffering each streamed Trevor reply into one assembled turn; **only** `run_conversation` is used (J7 is the conversation-only journey — no signature/avatar/export tools invoked).
- [schema] Each `run_conversation` call returns one assembled Trevor reply per turn (streamed `text_delta`/`extracted_fields` buffered into a single message), progressively populating the personal-brand fields (empathy map, mission, UVP blanks, tone adjectives, values, content calendar); no per-token streaming surfaced to the user.
- [skill-faithful] "a personal brand is how people perceive you (reputation, message, emotional connection), people trust individuals over a faceless entity, and in a crowded niche it is the differentiation fix" traces to 00-personal-brand-foundations.md; the four-box Thinks/Feels/Says/Does empathy map for ONE ideal person traces to 01-insight-audience-and-self/00-understand-your-audience.md; the two self-reflection sets (Set A passionate/sets-apart/message → Set B what/who/how-differ mission) + "insight = intersection of audience need and authentic offer" trace to 01-insight-audience-and-self/01-reflect-on-yourself.md; the UVP formula `I help [audience] achieve [outcome] by [approach]` + "avoid generic statements / be specific" rule + the book's small-business-owners worked example as the bar trace to 02-distinctive-and-empathetic/00-your-unique-value-proposition.md; the three-tone-adjectives portable filter (and the Canva/Adobe-Express style-guide aside) traces to 02-distinctive-and-empathetic/01-signature-style.md; "mirror how the audience talks, avoid jargon, plus the polls/Q&A listening loop" traces to 02-distinctive-and-empathetic/02-speak-their-language.md; the three-element story framework (challenge → steps → outcome-and-how-it-relates-to-their-journey, beat 3 enforced) traces to 02-distinctive-and-empathetic/03-share-relatable-stories.md; "cap values at three, each must block a real decision (sustainability example)" traces to 03-authentic/00-non-negotiable-values.md; "authenticity ≠ perfection; one honest post about a real failure + the lesson, paired with the 3-beat story" traces to 03-authentic/01-show-vulnerability.md; the 5-step strategy (reflect → platforms → 90-day content calendar → engage consistently → monitor & adapt) with the 90-day calendar as the one concrete deliverable traces to 04-personal-brand-strategy.md.
- [persona-adapt:P2] Coach TAUGHT foundations before applying — defined "personal brand," "UVP," "empathy map," "intersection," "vulnerability" before using each, and explicitly distinguished UVP vs. mission when Rico got confused; gave a worked example per concept (empathy-map first pass, mission draft, UVP draft, vulnerability post); supplied reusable fill-in templates (mission template, UVP formula, story template) and checklists (the specificity "could a competitor say it?" test, the speak-their-language do/don't table, the values "what does it block?" check); checked understanding repeatedly ("make sense so far?", "can you spot the difference?", "does the test make sense to run solo?"); patient and encouraging; leveraged Rico's abundant time with a thorough step-by-step multi-pillar exercise and an explicit numbered roadmap. Teaching + checklists/templates both present, and delivery is visibly more scaffolded than the P1 (Maya) compression run of this same journey.
- [artifact] Both promised deliverables present: a personal UVP with all three formula blanks filled and specificity-checked, plus a 90-day content plan (three months, every post tagged Story/Educational/Engagement, Story slots pre-structured with challenge→steps→outcome, day-90 monitor-and-adapt review).
- [safety] No cross-tenant data; every input is Dana's own (ASIN B0PET0005, the flat hero SKU, her vet-tech background, her dog Rufus, the anti-upsell stance) relayed through Rico from Dana's intake note, or a tool output; IV-OS canon read and asset write shown only as ⟦iv-os:⟧ capability tags, never invented as concrete records; no fabricated stats, certifications, clinical outcomes, or claims beyond the cited Ch10 skills — UVP/mission/values/stories are all built from Dana-supplied material, and the values block explicitly bars "miracle"/"reverses aging"/cure/"replaces the vet" claims.
